subsections.title: Scalability & Design Patterns
subsections.keyRule: System design interviews test: how to scale a system to millions of users. Key concepts: horizontal scaling, caching, database sharding, load balancing, message queues, CAP theorem. Always start with requirements, then estimate scale, then design.

terms.what: Sharding splits one large database table across multiple DB servers — each server holds a subset of the rows (a shard). Horizontal scaling for the database layer.
terms.why: Single DB server has limits (disk, CPU, connections). Sharding distributes data across many servers — scales write capacity and storage.
terms.how: Shard key (e.g., userId % 4) determines which shard. userId=0-24: shard 0. 25-49: shard 1. etc. Each shard is a separate DB instance.
terms.where: User data, order data at massive scale. MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB handle sharding natively.
terms.when: Add sharding when: single DB is at capacity, write volume exceeds single DB limit, data size exceeds single server disk.
terms.issue: Cache invalidation is hard — stale data served after DB update. Cache thundering herd — cache expires, thousands of requests hit DB simultaneously.
terms.fix: Short TTLs for frequently changing data. Use write-through for critical data. Add 'cache warming' on startup. Use probabilistic early expiration to prevent thundering herd.

star.s: Product page very slow under load.
star.a: Spring @Cacheable on product service. 5-minute TTL. On product update, @CacheEvict.
star.r: DB load reduced 90%. Page response from 200ms to 5ms. Database Sharding

terms.what: Load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple server instances. Prevents any single instance from being overwhelmed.
terms.why: Single server = single point of failure + limited capacity. Load balancer distributes load and provides high availability.
terms.how: Round-robin (default), least connections, IP hash (sticky sessions). Health checks: remove unhealthy instances automatically. AWS ALB, Nginx, HAProxy.
terms.where: In front of every horizontally scaled service tier. Web tier, API tier, microservices.
terms.when: Always use a load balancer for production. Even for single instance — makes scaling easy. Enables zero-downtime deployments.
terms.issue: Sticky sessions (IP hash) ties user to one server — if that server goes down, user's session is lost.
terms.fix: Store sessions externally (Redis). Use round-robin — any server can handle any user. No server-side session state.

star.s: During deployment, some users get errors.
star.a: Load balancer with health checks. Deploy one instance at a time (rolling deploy). LB drains connections before taking instance out.

terms.what: CAP: Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition tolerance (system works despite network partition). You can only guarantee two of three.
terms.why: Forces you to think about trade-offs in distributed systems. No system can have perfect consistency, availability, AND partition tolerance simultaneously.
terms.how: CP (Consistency + Partition): HBase, ZooKeeper — sacrifices availability during partition. AP (Availability + Partition): DynamoDB, Cassandra — sacrifices strict consistency (eventual consistency). CA systems: traditional single-server DB (no partition tolerance).
terms.where: Distributed system design decisions: choosing between MySQL (CP) and Cassandra (AP).
terms.when: For financial systems (need strong consistency): CP. For social media, analytics (can tolerate eventual consistency): AP. Real systems are more nuanced — PACELC theorem extends CAP.
terms.issue: Claiming a system is 'CA' in a distributed environment — network partitions WILL happen. CA is not realistic in distributed systems.
terms.fix: Design for partition tolerance always. Choose C vs A based on your use case.

star.a: CP system (strong consistency) — cannot lose a transaction or show wrong balance. Accept reduced availability over wrong data.

terms.what: Message queue decouples services — producer sends message and continues. Consumer processes asynchronously. Queue holds messages until consumed.
terms.why: Synchronous service calls create tight coupling. If consumer is slow/down, producer slows/fails too. Queue absorbs spikes and isolates failures.
terms.how: Producer → Queue (Kafka/SQS/RabbitMQ) → Consumer. Producer does not wait for consumer. Queue persists messages during consumer downtime.
terms.where: Order placed → queue → inventory update, email, loyalty points (all async). Background jobs, event-driven microservices.
terms.when: Use queues when: operations can be async, consumer slower than producer (back- pressure), one event must trigger multiple consumers.
terms.issue: If consumer fails and no retry/DLQ configured — messages silently lost.

subsections.title: Clean Code & Performance
subsections.keyRule: Clean code = readable, simple, intention-revealing. Good names > comments. Short methods > long methods. One responsibility per method. Fail fast with early returns. Avoid magic numbers — use named constants. Review code with the question: 'Will I understand this in 6 months?'

terms.what: SOLID principles applied in real code: inject dependencies, one responsibility per class, code to interfaces, small interfaces, extend via composition not modification.
terms.why: SOLID code is easy to test (DI), easy to extend (OCP), easy to change (SRP), safe to refactor (LSP).
terms.how: SRP: OrderService only processes orders — not sends emails (EmailService does that). DIP: inject OrderRepository interface. OCP: add new discount type by adding new class.
terms.where: Service design, class design, API design. Spring application architecture follows SOLID naturally.
terms.when: Apply consciously when designing classes. Review violations during code review.
terms.issue: A class that violates SRP is a sign it has too many dependencies — sign in constructor with 8 injected dependencies.
terms.fix: If constructor has more than 4-5 dependencies, the class likely does too much. Split responsibility.

star.a: Split: OrderValidationService, OrderPricingService, OrderPersistenceService, OrderNotificationService. Each has 2-3 dependencies.

terms.what: Systematic process: measure first (profiler), identify bottleneck, fix the bottleneck, measure again. Never optimize without measurement.
terms.why: Premature optimization wastes time on the wrong thing. Measurement reveals the actual bottleneck — usually not where you expect.
terms.how: Tools: JProfiler, YourKit, async-profiler, Spring Boot Actuator /metrics. Steps: benchmark baseline → profile → find hotspot → fix → benchmark again.
terms.where: Performance issues in: DB queries (N+1, missing index), serialization, string building, algorithmic complexity.
terms.when: Optimize when: measured evidence of performance problem. Not before. Profile first — 80% of time is spent in 20% of code.

terms.what: Logging records runtime behavior for debugging and monitoring. Good logging makes production issues diagnosable. Bad logging either hides problems or floods with noise.
terms.why: In production, logging is often the only visibility you have. Cannot attach debugger to production. Logs must tell the full story.
terms.how: Use SLF4J + Logback/Log4j2. Levels: ERROR (needs immediate action), WARN (potential problem), INFO (normal events), DEBUG (dev only). Parameterized: log.info('User {} logged in', userId) not String concat.
terms.where: Service entry/exit points, external calls (request+response), business events (order placed), error handling (with full exception).
terms.when: INFO for business events. DEBUG for technical details (disabled in production). Always log exceptions with full stack trace: log.error('Failed', e). Never swallow exceptions silently.
terms.issue: log.error('Failed: ' + e.getMessage()) — loses stack trace. String concatenation in disabled log level still runs.
terms.fix: log.error('Failed to process order {}', orderId, e) — third argument is the Throwable, logs full stack trace. Parameterized logging is lazy — no string building if level disabled.

star.s: Production exception 'Failed to save' with no stack trace — impossible to debug.
star.a: Found catch block logging e.getMessage() only. Changed to log.error('Failed to save user {}', userId, e).
star.r: Full stack trace in logs. Issues diagnosable.

subsections.title: Maven, Docker & CI/CD
subsections.keyRule: Maven/Gradle = build tools (compile, test, package). Docker = containerize your app into a portable image. CI/CD = automate build, test, and deploy on every commit. These three together form the modern Java delivery pipeline.

terms.what: Maven is a build tool and dependency manager. It compiles code, runs tests, packages into JAR/WAR, and manages third-party dependencies from repositories.
terms.why: Without a build tool, managing 50+ dependencies manually (version conflicts, transitive deps) is impossible. Maven handles this with pom.xml.
terms.how: mvn package — compile + test + create JAR. mvn test — compile + test. mvn clean — delete target/. mvn dependency:tree — see dependency tree. Lifecycle: validate → compile → test → package → verify → install → deploy.
terms.where: Every Java project. pom.xml defines all dependencies, build plugins, and project structure.
terms.when: Use Maven or Gradle. Gradle is faster (incremental builds, parallel tasks). Maven is more common in enterprise. Both are fine.
terms.issue: Dependency version conflicts: two libraries need different versions of the same transitive dependency.
terms.fix: mvn dependency:tree to find conflicts. Use <dependencyManagement> to force a specific version for all transitive dependencies.

terms.what: Kubernetes (K8s) orchestrates Docker containers at scale — manages deployment, scaling, rolling updates, health checks, and self-healing.
terms.why: Running 50 microservices manually is impossible. Kubernetes automates: restart crashed containers, scale on load, rolling deploy with no downtime.
terms.how: Deployment YAML specifies: Docker image, replicas, resource limits, health checks. Service YAML exposes the app. Apply: kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml.
terms.where: Every modern cloud deployment. GKE (Google), EKS (Amazon), AKS (Azure) are managed Kubernetes.
terms.when: Use Kubernetes when: multiple microservices, need auto-scaling, need zero- downtime deploys, cloud-native architecture.
terms.issue: Not setting resource limits (CPU/memory) on pods — one misbehaving pod takes down the entire node.
terms.fix: Always set resources: requests (guaranteed) and limits (maximum) for every pod. JVM -Xmx must be less than pod memory limit.

star.s: One pod consuming all node memory causes other pods to be killed.
star.a: Set resource limits on all pods. Set Java -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 to respect container limit.
star.r: Pods isolated. No more neighbor evictions.

terms.what: Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready endpoints: /health, /metrics, /info, /loggers, /threaddump, /heapdump. Kubernetes uses /health for liveness and readiness probes.
terms.why: Kubernetes needs to know if your app is healthy. /actuator/health tells it. Metrics endpoints feed monitoring dashboards (Prometheus + Grafana).
terms.how: Add spring-boot-actuator dependency. /actuator/health — up/down. /actuator/metrics — JVM, HTTP, DB metrics. Expose to Prometheus for monitoring.
terms.where: Every Spring Boot app in production. Kubernetes config references health endpoint for probes.
terms.when: Always add Actuator. Secure /actuator endpoints (not publicly accessible). Expose metrics to Prometheus.
terms.issue: Liveness probe failing because app initializing slowly — Kubernetes kills the container before it is ready.
terms.fix: Use separate readiness probe (is app ready to serve?) vs liveness probe (is app alive?). Readiness probe can fail during startup. Add startupProbe for slow-starting apps.

star.s: Kubernetes killing pods during startup — app takes 30 seconds to initialize.
star.a: Added startupProbe with failureThreshold=30 (allows 30s). ReadinessProbe separate from LivenessProbe.
star.r: Pods given time to start. No premature kills.

subsections.title: Redis Deep Dive
subsections.keyRule: Redis: in-memory key-value store. TTL for expiry. LRU eviction when memory full. Sentinel for HA failover. Cluster for horizontal scaling. Pub/Sub for messaging. SETNX for distributed locks. Beware cache stampede with jitter. Spring @Cacheable for transparent caching.

terms.what: TTL (Time-To-Live): EXPIRE key 3600 — key auto-deleted after N seconds. Eviction policies control what to delete when memory is full: allkeys-lru (evict least recently used from all keys), volatile-lru (evict LRU from keys with TTL), noeviction (reject writes — safer).
terms.why: TTL prevents stale data. Eviction prevents OOM when cache grows beyond memory limit.
terms.how: EXPIRE key 300. TTL key → remaining seconds. Eviction: set maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru in redis.conf.
terms.where: All cache entries should have TTL. Eviction policy for session caches, general caches.
terms.when: Always set TTL on cache entries. Choose eviction based on use: allkeys-lru for general cache, volatile-lru when only some keys should be evictable.
terms.issue: No TTL + noeviction policy = Redis fills up and throws OOM error for all writes.
terms.fix: Set appropriate TTL for all cache entries. Set eviction policy. Monitor Redis memory usage.

star.s: Redis fills up — new cache writes fail.
star.a: Added EXPIRE to all cache SET operations. Set maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru.
star.r: Old entries evicted automatically. Redis never full.

terms.what: Redis Sentinel: monitors master, detects failure, performs automatic failover to replica. High availability. Redis Cluster: data sharded across multiple nodes — horizontal scaling beyond single-node memory.
terms.why: Single Redis = single point of failure. Sentinel for HA. Cluster for data larger than one node.
terms.how: Sentinel: 3 sentinel processes monitor master. If master down, sentinels elect new master from replicas. Cluster: data split across 16384 hash slots distributed across nodes.
terms.where: Production Redis setups. Sentinel for most apps. Cluster for truly large datasets (>100GB).
terms.when: Use Sentinel for HA. Use Cluster when data exceeds single node capacity.
terms.issue: Redis Cluster: multi-key operations (MGET, MSET) only work if all keys are in the same slot (same hash tag).
terms.fix: Use hash tags {userId}:session and {userId}:cart to force related keys to same slot.

star.s: Redis single node — one failure = all cache gone.
star.a: Redis Sentinel with 1 master + 2 replicas + 3 sentinels.
star.r: Automatic failover. No cache downtime on node failure.

terms.what: Redis Pub/Sub: publish to channel, subscribers receive — not durable, no acknowledgment. Redis Streams: durable log with consumer groups, acknowledgments — like lightweight Kafka.
terms.why: Pub/Sub for real-time notifications where loss is acceptable. Streams for reliable message delivery where each message must be processed.
terms.how: Pub/Sub: PUBLISH channel message. SUBSCRIBE channel. Streams: XADD stream * key value. XREADGROUP GROUP mygroup consumer STREAMS stream.
terms.where: Pub/Sub: real-time chat, live notifications, score updates. Streams: reliable event processing, audit logs.
terms.when: Pub/Sub: fire-and-forget real-time events. Streams: when you need delivery guarantees but Kafka is too heavy.
terms.issue: Pub/Sub: if subscriber not connected when message published, message is lost forever.

terms.what: Distributed lock: SET key value NX PX timeout — atomic set-if-not-exists with expiry. Cache stampede: all cache entries expire simultaneously — all requests hit DB at once.
terms.why: Distributed lock for leader election, resource exclusion across services. Cache stampede protection for high-traffic systems.
terms.how: Lock: SET lock:resource uuid NX PX 30000. If null → locked by others. Release: if (get(lock)==uuid) del(lock). Stampede fix: random TTL jitter (TTL + random(0,300s)).
terms.where: Distributed lock: payment deduplication, job scheduling, critical section across services. Stampede: product pages, homepage, any popular cached data.
terms.when: Use distributed lock for cross-service mutual exclusion. Use jitter to prevent stampede on regularly refreshed caches.
terms.issue: Lock expiry before operation completes — another process gets lock while first still holding it.
terms.fix: Extend lock TTL if operation is running longer than expected. Use Redisson library — handles lock extension automatically.

star.s: Cache for product page set to expire every midnight — all 1M users hit DB simultaneously at midnight.
star.a: Added ±30 minute random jitter to TTL.
star.r: Expiries spread over 1-hour window. DB load normalized.

terms.what: @Cacheable: method result cached on first call, subsequent calls return cached value. @CacheEvict: removes cache entry. @CachePut: always updates cache. Backed by Redis, Ehcache, Caffeine.
terms.why: Transparent caching — add annotation, Spring handles cache logic. No manual get/set/evict code.
terms.how: @Cacheable(value='users', key='#id') User findUser(Long id). @CacheEvict(value='users', key='#user.id') void updateUser(User user). Spring auto- configures Redis if spring-data-redis is on classpath.
terms.where: Service methods returning data that is: read frequently, expensive to compute, rarely changes.
terms.when: Use @Cacheable for expensive reads. Add @CacheEvict on all write methods that invalidate cached data.
terms.issue: @CacheEvict missed — data updated in DB but old value still in cache. Users see stale data.
terms.fix: Add @CacheEvict on ALL write operations for cached data. Or use @Caching to combine multiple cache operations.

star.s: Product page slow — DB hit on every request.
star.r: 95% requests served from cache. Page load: 500ms → 20ms.

terms.what: Normalization: organize tables to reduce redundancy (1NF → 2NF → 3NF). Denormalization: intentional redundancy for read performance — duplicate data to avoid joins.
terms.why: Normalized DB: consistent, no update anomalies. Denormalized: fewer joins = faster reads for reporting/analytics.
terms.how: 3NF: no transitive dependencies. Customer table has customer columns only. Address in separate table. Denormalized: reporting table has customer + address + order columns all together.

terms.what: Composite index: index on multiple columns (col1, col2). Order matters — queries filtering on col1 use the index; col2 alone does not. Covering index: index contains all columns needed by query — no table row access.
terms.why: Right index = O(log n) query. Wrong or missing index = full table scan = O(n).
terms.how: CREATE INDEX idx_user_dept ON users(department_id, hire_date) — queries on department_id use this. CREATE INDEX idx_cover ON orders(customer_id, status, total) — covers SELECT status,total WHERE customer_id=?
terms.where: High-traffic query paths, foreign keys, columns in WHERE/ORDER BY/GROUP BY.
terms.when: Add index based on actual slow queries (EXPLAIN output). Do NOT add indexes speculatively — too many indexes slow writes.
terms.issue: Too many indexes: each INSERT/UPDATE must update all indexes — slows writes significantly.
terms.fix: Index only what you measure is slow. Composite index order: highest selectivity first. Remove unused indexes.

star.s: ORDER query with WHERE customer_id AND status takes 2 seconds.
star.a: Composite index (customer_id, status). EXPLAIN shows index scan.

terms.what: EXPLAIN shows query execution plan — whether index is used, estimated rows, join type. Optimistic locking: @Version in JPA — update fails if another transaction changed the row. Pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE — DB row lock.
terms.why: EXPLAIN reveals full table scans before they hit production. Optimistic locking: no DB lock held — better concurrency. Pessimistic locking: guarantees no concurrent modification — safe but slow.
terms.how: EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ... — shows actual timing. @Version Long version; on entity — JPA checks version matches on UPDATE. @Lock(PESSIMISTIC_WRITE) on query — holds row lock until commit.
terms.where: EXPLAIN: query tuning, index verification. Optimistic: high-read low-write scenarios. Pessimistic: financial transactions, inventory deduction.
terms.when: Use optimistic locking by default. Switch to pessimistic only when concurrent conflicts are frequent and optimistic retry is too expensive.
terms.issue: Optimistic locking: high contention = many OptimisticLockExceptions = many retries = slow.
terms.fix: For high-contention (multiple users updating same row): pessimistic lock. For low- contention: optimistic.

star.a: Pessimistic locking: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on inventory row. Update only if quantity >= requested.

terms.what: Read replica: separate DB instance synced from primary — serves reads only, offloads read traffic from primary. Polyglot persistence: use different DB technologies for different services.
terms.why: Read replica: most apps are read-heavy. Separate read and write paths for scalability. Polyglot: use the right DB for each use case — relational for transactions, Redis for cache, MongoDB for documents.
